inputs.story: Several marathon runners decided to carb-load at Jimmy's Italian Restaurant on the night before the big run. Match each runner to the type of pasta and sauce they ordered and the total price of their entree. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
inputs.constraints:
1. Suzanne paid less than Margie.
2. Margie paid $7.
3. The competitor who ordered spaghetti paid 2 dollars more than the competitor who ordered taglioni.
4. Of the runner who paid $9 and the contestant who ordered fettuccine, one was Margie and the other was Velma.
inputs.constants:
prices: $6; $7; $8; $9.
runners: Florence; Margie; Suzanne; Velma.
pastas: fettuccine; fusilli; spaghetti; taglioni.
inputs.solution:
Prices	Runners	Pastas
$6	Suzanne	taglioni
$7	Margie	fettuccine
$8	Florence	spaghetti
$9	Velma	fusilli

asp_generation.prompt:
<task>
You are given a logic grid puzzle with a story, numbered clues, and categorized constants. Your task is to generate a complete ASP (Answer Set Programming) program that, when solved with Clingo, yields a unique answer set representing the puzzle's solution.

Provided below are:
- two examples: `example_1` and `example_2`,
- the problem you must solve: `problem_to_solve`,
- important rules: `rules`.
</task>

<example_1>
"Against the Grain" offers hand-made wooden furniture at reasonable prices. Each item is made by an in-house employee. Using only the clues that follow, match each item to the employee who crafted it, and determine its price and the type of wood used to make it. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Constants:
Employees: Bonita; Yvette; Tabitha.
Prices: $225; $275; $325.
Wood types: ash; poplar; sandalwood.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
employee("Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha").
price(225; 275; 325).
wood_type("ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood").

% Search space: for each employee E, assign exactly one price P and wood type W.
{match(E, P, W): price(P), wood_type(W)}=1 :- employee(E).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; P1=P2; W1=W2}=0 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), (E1,P1,W1)!=(E2,P2,W2).

% 1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
P=325 :- match(E,P,W), E="Bonita".

% 2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
P1>P2 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), W1="poplar", E2="Yvette".

% 3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
P1=P2-50 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), E1="Tabitha", W2="sandalwood".

% 4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.
{W="ash"; E="Yvette"}=1 :- match(E,P,W), P=275.

#show match/3.
```
</example_1>

<example_2>
The Winter Olympics have just wrapped up in Norway. Using only the clues that follow, determine the number of gold and silver medals won by each country. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The four teams are the team from Bolivia, the team that won 3 gold medals, the team that won 6 silver medals, and the team from Argentina.
2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams.
5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.

Constants:
Countries: Argentina; Bolivia; Grenada; Oman.
Silver medals: 2; 6; 10; 11.
Gold medals: 1; 2; 3; 4.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
country("Argentina"; "Bolivia"; "Grenada"; "Oman").
silver_medals(2; 6; 10; 11).
gold_medals(1; 2; 3; 4).

% Search space: for each country C, assign exactly one silver medal count S and gold medal count G.
{assign(C, S, G): silver_medals(S), gold_medals(G)}=1 :- country(C).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{C1=C2; S1=S2; G1=G2}=0 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), (C1,S1,G1)!=(C2,S2,G2).

% 1. The four teams are all different — Bolivia, the 3-gold team, the 6-silver team, and Argentina each refer to distinct countries.
{C="Bolivia"; G=3; S=6; C="Argentina"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G).

% 2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Oman", S2=10.

% 3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
{G=2; G=1}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), C="Oman".

% 4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams — three pairwise inequalities.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C2!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C1!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".

% 5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
{C="Argentina"; S=2}=0 :- assign(C,S,G), G=4.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Argentina", S2=2.

% 6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.
{S=6; C="Grenada"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), G=2.

#show assign/3.
```
</example_2>

Here is the problem to be solved:
<problem_to_solve>
Several marathon runners decided to carb-load at Jimmy's Italian Restaurant on the night before the big run. Match each runner to the type of pasta and sauce they ordered and the total price of their entree. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Suzanne paid less than Margie.
2. Margie paid $7.
3. The competitor who ordered spaghetti paid 2 dollars more than the competitor who ordered taglioni.
4. Of the runner who paid $9 and the contestant who ordered fettuccine, one was Margie and the other was Velma.

Constants:
prices: $6; $7; $8; $9.
runners: Florence; Margie; Suzanne; Velma.
pastas: fettuccine; fusilli; spaghetti; taglioni.
</problem_to_solve>

Here are some important rules to follow when generating the ASP code:
<rules>
1. Constant declarations — "category(const1; const2; ...)."
   - Category names: lowercase with underscores only (e.g., wood_type, gold_medals).
   - All constants in the same category must use the same format — all integers or all quoted strings.
   - Prefer integers: strip special characters ($225 → 225), strip units while keeping the number ("3 days" → 3, "2 months" → 2), and convert verbal or ordinal numerals (once → 1, twice → 2, third → 3, September → 9).
   - Use double-quoted strings when no integer representation is possible (e.g., "Bonita", "9:30AM").

2. Predicates
   - Use the minimum number of predicates needed to relate all categories.
   - Every category must appear in at least one predicate argument; all arguments must be distinct variables.
   - Choose a name that reflects the relationship (e.g., match, assign, schedule, launch).

3. Search space — one choice rule per predicate, anchoring on one category in the body and ranging over the rest inside the braces:
     {pred(V1, V2, ...): cat2(V2), cat3(V3), ...}=1 :- cat1(V1).

4. Uniqueness — no value in any non-anchor category appears more than once:
     {V1=V2; W1=W2; ...}=0 :- pred(V1,W1,...), pred(V2,W2,...), (V1,W1,...)!=(V2,W2,...).

5. Constraint rules — encode each clue using one or more rules of these forms, using only defined constants and predicates:
     <C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.      % if all Li hold, at least one Ci must hold
     {<C1>; ...; <Cm>}=k :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.  % if all Li hold, exactly k of Ci hold
     :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.                        % this combination is forbidden

   Common patterns:
   - "Of A and B, one is C and the other is D" → three rules: A≠B, A is C or D, B is C or D.
   - "A, B and C are all different" → three pairwise inequality rules (A≠B, A≠C, B≠C).
   - "Neither A nor B is X" → {A_cond; B_cond}=0 :- ... X ... .

6. Show directives — one "#show pred/arity." per predicate.
</rules>

ASP Program:
asp_generation.thinking:
Here's a thinking process:

1.  **Understand the Goal**: The user wants an ASP (Answer Set Programming) program that solves a specific logic grid puzzle. The program should be written in a format compatible with Clingo, following the provided examples and rules.

2.  **Analyze the Problem**:
   - **Story**: Marathon runners at a restaurant. Match each runner to a pasta type and a price.
   - **Clues**:
     1. Suzanne paid less than Margie.
     2. Margie paid $7.
     3. The competitor who ordered spaghetti paid 2 dollars more than the competitor who ordered taglioni.
     4. Of the runner who paid $9 and the contestant who ordered fettuccine, one was Margie and the other was Velma.
   - **Constants**:
     - Prices: $6; $7; $8; $9. (Strip $: 6, 7, 8, 9)
     - Runners: Florence; Margie; Suzanne; Velma.
     - Pastas: fettuccine; fusilli; spaghetti; taglioni.
   - **Goal**: Unique assignment of (Runner, Pasta, Price).

3.  **Map Constants to ASP Format**:
   - `price(6; 7; 8; 9).`
   - `runner("Florence"; "Margie"; "Suzanne"; "Velma").`
   - `pasta("fettuccine"; "fusilli"; "spaghetti"; "taglioni").`

4.  **Define Predicates**:
   - I'll use `order(Runner, Pasta, Price)` to represent the assignment.
   - Search space: `{order(R, P, Price): pasta(P), price(Price)}=1 :- runner(R).`
   - Uniqueness: `{R1=R2; P1=P2; Price1=Price2}=0 :- order(R1,P1,Price1), order(R2,P2,Price2), (R1,P1,Price1)!=(R2,P2,Price2).`

5.  **Translate Clues to ASP**:
   - *Clue 1*: Suzanne paid less than Margie.
     - `Price_Suzanne < Price_Margie`
     - ASP: `Price1 < Price2 :- order("Suzanne", _, Price1), order("Margie", _, Price2).`
   - *Clue 2*: Margie paid $7.
     - `order("Margie", _, 7).` or `Price=7 :- order("Margie", _, Price).` (The example uses `P=7 :- match(E,P,W), E="Margie".` or similar. I'll stick to `Price=7 :- order("Margie", P, Price).` or just `order("Margie", _, 7).` Actually, the example uses `P=325 :- match(E,P,W), E="Bonita".` I'll follow that style: `Price=7 :- order("Margie", _, Price).`)
   - *Clue 3*: The competitor who ordered spaghetti paid 2 dollars more than the competitor who ordered taglioni.
     - `Price_spaghetti = Price_taglioni + 2`
     - ASP: `Price1 = Price2 + 2 :- order(_, "spaghetti", Price1), order(_, "taglioni", Price2).`
   - *Clue 4*: Of the runner who paid $9 and the contestant who ordered fettuccine, one was Margie and the other was Velma.
     - This means: {Runner who paid 9 is Margie, Runner who paid 9 is Velma} is not quite right. It means the two entities (the $9 payer, the fettuccine eater) are exactly Margie and Velma in some order.
     - So: `order("Margie", _, 9).` or `order("Margie", "fettuccine", _).` (exactly one of these)
     - And: `order("Velma", _, 9).` or `order("Velma", "fettuccine", _).` (exactly one of these)
     - Also, they must be different people (already guaranteed by uniqueness, but we can add constraints if needed, though the "one is... other is..." pattern in Example 2 is encoded as:
       `{S=6; C="Grenada"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), G=2.` for a similar structure.
       Let's adapt Example 2's pattern for clue 4:
       "Of the runner who paid $9 and the contestant who ordered fettuccine, one was Margie and the other was Velma."
       This implies:
       - The person who paid $9 is either Margie or Velma.
       - The person who ordered fettuccine is either Margie or Velma.
       - They are distinct (one is Margie, the other is Velma).
       Actually, Example 2 clue 6: "The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada." -> `{S=6; C="Grenada"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), G=2.`
       For clue 4, it's slightly different: It's about two specific roles (paid $9, ordered fettuccine) mapping to two specific people (Margie, Velma).
       Let's break it down logically:
       1. `order("Margie", _, 9) ; order("Margie", "fettuccine", _)` is true.
       2. `order("Velma", _, 9) ; order("Velma", "fettuccine", _)` is true.
       3. The $9 payer and fettuccine eater are different runners. (Already implied if one is Margie and one is Velma, but we can enforce `P1!=P2` for the respective assignments, or just rely on the fact that Margie != Velma).
       Actually, the standard way to encode "Of A and B, one is C and the other is D" in these puzzles is:
       `C is A or D is A` -> `{A_cond_C; A_cond_D}=1`? Wait.
       Let's look at the exact wording: "Of the runner who paid $9 and the contestant who ordered fettuccine, one was Margie and the other was Velma."
       This means:
       - Runner($9) is either Margie or Velma.
       - Runner(fettuccine) is either Margie or Velma.
       - Runner($9) != Runner(fettuccine).
       So:
       `{P="Margie"; P="Velma"}=1 :- order(P, _, 9).`
       `{P="Margie"; P="Velma"}=1 :- order(P, "fettuccine", _).`
       `order(P1, _, 9), order(P2, "fettuccine", _) :- P1!=P2.` (Constraint to ensure they are different)
       Wait, the constraint `:- order(P1, _, 9), order(P1, "fettuccine", _).` ensures they are different runners.
       Let's verify with Example 2 clue 6: "The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada." -> `{S=6; C="Grenada"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), G=2.` This only assigns one role to two possibilities.
       Clue 4 has two roles and two people. I will use:
       `{P="Margie"; P="Velma"}=1 :- order(P, _, 9).`
       `{P="Margie"; P="Velma"}=1 :- order(P, "fettuccine", _).`
       `:- order(P1, _, 9), order(P1, "fettuccine", _).` (They can't be the same person)
       This matches "one was Margie and the other was Velma".

   Let's double check clue 3: "The competitor who ordered spaghetti paid 2 dollars more than the competitor who ordered taglioni."
   `Price_spag = Price_tag + 2`.
   Prices are 6,7,8,9.
   Possible pairs: (8,6). So spaghetti=8, taglioni=6.
   Clue 2: Margie=7.
   Clue 1: Suzanne < Margie(7) => Suzanne=6.
   So Suzanne=6, taglioni=6 => Suzanne ordered taglioni.
   Spaghetti=8.
   Margie=7.
   Velma/Florence left for 8 and 9.
   Clue 4: $9 and fettuccine are Margie and Velma.
   Margie=7, so Margie is not $9. Thus Margie is fettuccine.
   So Velma is $9.
   Then Florence is $8 and spaghetti.
   Let's verify:
   Suzanne: 6, taglioni
   Margie: 7, fettuccine
   Florence: 8, spaghetti
   Velma: 9, fusilli
   Checks out.

   Now, encode clues carefully in ASP.
   Clue 1: `Price1 < Price2 :- order("Suzanne", _, Price1), order("Margie", _, Price2).`
   Clue 2: `Price=7 :- order("Margie", _, Price).`
   Clue 3: `Price1 = Price2 + 2 :- order(_, "spaghetti", Price1), order(_, "taglioni", Price2).`
   Clue 4:
   `{P="Margie"; P="Velma"}=1 :- order(P, _, 9).`
   `{P="Margie"; P="Velma"}=1 :- order(P, "fettuccine", _).`
   `:- order(P, _, 9), order(P, "fettuccine", _).`

   Wait, is there a more compact way for Clue 4 following the examples?
   Example 2 clue 6: `{S=6; C="Grenada"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), G=2.`
   For clue 4, it's about two different attributes mapping to two different constants.
   I'll stick with my formulation. It's clear and correct.
   Alternatively, `:- order(M, _, 9), order(M, "fettuccine", _).` (same person can't be both)
   `{P="Margie"; P="Velma"}=1 :- order(P, _, 9).`
   `{P="Margie"; P="Velma"}=1 :- order(P, "fettuccine", _).`
   This is fine.

   Let's review the rules for encoding clues:
   `<C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.`
   `{<C1>; ...; <Cm>}=k :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.`
   `:- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.`

   For Clue 4, I can also write:
   `order("Margie", _, 9) ; order("Margie", "fettuccine", _)` is not directly supported as a head, but `{order("Margie", _, 9); order("Margie", "fettuccine", _)}=1` would work if we treat them as atoms, but they are part of the same predicate. Better to use body conditions:
   `{P="Margie"; P="Velma"}=1 :- order(P, _, 9).`
   `{P="Margie"; P="Velma"}=1 :- order(P, "fettuccine", _).`
   `:- order(P1, _, 9), order(P2, "fettuccine", _), P1=P2.`

   This matches the pattern.

   Let's assemble the full program.

   ```asp
   % Constants
   price(6; 7; 8; 9).
   runner("Florence"; "Margie"; "Suzanne"; "Velma").
   pasta("fettuccine"; "fusilli"; "spaghetti"; "taglioni").

   % Search space
   {order(R, P, Pr): pasta(P), price(Pr)}=1 :- runner(R).

   % Uniqueness
   {R1=R2; P1=P2; Pr1=Pr2}=0 :- order(R1,P1,Pr1), order(R2,P2,Pr2), (R1,P1,Pr1)!=(R2,P2,Pr2).

   % 1. Suzanne paid less than Margie.
   Pr1 < Pr2 :- order("Suzanne", _, Pr1), order("Margie", _, Pr2).

   % 2. Margie paid $7.
   Pr=7 :- order("Margie", _, Pr).

   % 3. The competitor who ordered spaghetti paid 2 dollars more than the competitor who ordered taglioni.
   Pr1 = Pr2 + 2 :- order(_, "spaghetti", Pr1), order(_, "taglioni", Pr2).

   % 4. Of the runner who paid $9 and the contestant who ordered fettuccine, one was Margie and the other was Velma.
   {P="Margie"; P="Velma"}=1 :- order(P, _, 9).
   {P="Margie"; P="Velma"}=1 :- order(P, "fettuccine", _).
   :- order(P1, _, 9), order(P2, "fettuccine", _), P1=P2.

   #show order/3.
   ```

   Check rules compliance:
   - Constant declarations: `price(6; 7; 8; 9).` (integers, stripped $)
   - `runner("Florence"; "Margie"; "Suzanne"; "Velma").`
   - `pasta("fettuccine"; "fusilli"; "spaghetti"; "taglioni").`
   - Predicates: `order/3` used.
   - Search space: `{order(R, P, Pr): pasta(P), price(Pr)}=1 :- runner(R).` matches rule 3.
   - Uniqueness: matches rule 4.
   - Clue 1: `Pr1 < Pr2 :- order("Suzanne", _, Pr1), order("Margie", _, Pr2).` -> valid.
   - Clue 2: `Pr=7 :- order("Margie", _, Pr).` -> valid.
   - Clue 3: `Pr1 = Pr2 + 2 :- order(_, "spaghetti", Pr1), order(_, "taglioni", Pr2).` -> valid.
   - Clue 4: The three rules handle the "one is... other is..." pattern. It ensures exactly one of Margie/Velma paid $9, exactly one ordered fettuccine, and they are not the same person. This correctly enforces that the $9 payer and fettuccine eater are exactly Margie and Velma.
   - Show directive: `#show order/3.`

   One minor point: In Example 1, clue 4 says "The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item." -> `{W="ash"; E="Yvette"}=1 :- match(E,P,W), P=275.`
   My clue 4 is slightly different but logically sound. I'll stick with it.

   Let's verify if there's any edge case with Clue 4 encoding.
   "Of the runner who paid $9 and the contestant who ordered fettuccine, one was Margie and the other was Velma."
   This implies:
   - The $9 runner is either Margie or Velma.
   - The fettuccine runner is either Margie or Velma.
   - They are different people.
   My rules:
   `{P="Margie"; P="Velma"}=1 :- order(P, _, 9).` (Exactly one of M/V paid 9)
   `{P="Margie"; P="Velma"}=1 :- order(P, "fettuccine", _).` (Exactly one of M/V ate fettuccine)
   `:- order(P1, _, 9), order(P2, "fettuccine", _), P1=P2.` (They are different)
   This is correct and robust.

   Could also write clue 4 as:
   `order("Margie", _, 9) ; order("Margie", "fettuccine", _)` is not valid ASP head.
   The set constraint approach is standard.

   Wait, rule 5 says: "Common patterns: 'Of A and B, one is C and the other is D' → three rules: A≠B, A is C or D, B is C or D."
   In the problem, A = runner who paid $9, B = contestant who ordered fettuccine. C = Margie, D = Velma.
   So:
   A ≠ B → `:- order(P, _, 9), order(P, "fettuccine", _).`
   A is C or D → `{P="Margie"; P="Velma"}=1 :- order(P, _, 9).`
   B is C or D → `{P="Margie"; P="Velma"}=1 :- order(P, "fettuccine", _).`
   This exactly matches the instruction! Perfect.

   Check variable naming in uniqueness rule: `{R1=R2; P1=P2; Pr1=Pr2}=0 :- order(R1,P1,Pr1), order(R2,P2,Pr2), (R1,P1,Pr1)!=(R2,P2,Pr2).`
   This is correct.

   Check search space rule: `{order(R, P, Pr): pasta(P), price(Pr)}=1 :- runner(R).`
   Correct.

   All constants are correctly formatted.
   Prices: 6, 7, 8, 9.
   Runners: "Florence", "Margie", "Suzanne", "Velma".
   Pastas: "fettuccine", "fusilli", "spaghetti", "taglioni".

   One detail: In Example 1, clue 1 is `P=325 :- match(E,P,W), E="Bonita".`
   I used `Pr=7 :- order("Margie", _, Pr).` which is fine.

   Let's assemble the final code carefully.

   ```asp
   % Constants
   price(6; 7; 8; 9).
   runner("Florence"; "Margie"; "Suzanne"; "Velma").
   pasta("fettuccine"; "fusilli"; "spaghetti"; "taglioni").

   % Search space
   {order(R, P, Pr): pasta(P), price(Pr)}=1 :- runner(R).

   % Uniqueness
   {R1=R2; P1=P2; Pr1=Pr2}=0 :- order(R1,P1,Pr1), order(R2,P2,Pr2), (R1,P1,Pr1)!=(R2,P2,Pr2).

   % 1. Suzanne paid less than Margie.
   Pr1 < Pr2 :- order("Suzanne", _, Pr1), order("Margie", _, Pr2).

   % 2. Margie paid $7.
   Pr=7 :- order("Margie", _, Pr).

   % 3. The competitor who ordered spaghetti paid 2 dollars more than the competitor who ordered taglioni.
   Pr1 = Pr2 + 2 :- order(_, "spaghetti", Pr1), order(_, "taglioni", Pr2).

   % 4. Of the runner who paid $9 and the contestant who ordered fettuccine, one was Margie and the other was Velma.
   {P="Margie"; P="Velma"}=1 :- order(P, _, 9).
   {P="Margie"; P="Velma"}=1 :- order(P, "fettuccine", _).
   :- order(P1, _, 9), order(P2, "fettuccine", _), P1=P2.

   #show order/3.
   ```

   Wait, should I use `match` or `order`? The prompt says "Choose a name that reflects the relationship (e.g., match, assign, schedule, launch)." `order` is fine. I'll stick with `order`.

   Check rule 3: "Search space — one choice rule per predicate, anchoring on one category in the body and ranging over the rest inside the braces: `{pred(V1, V2, ...): cat2(V2), cat3(V3), ...}=1 :- cat1(V1).`"

pasta("fettuccine"; "fusilli"; "spaghetti"; "taglioni").

% 1. Suzanne paid less than Margie.
Pr1 < Pr2 :- order("Suzanne", _, Pr1), order("Margie", _, Pr2).

% 2. Margie paid $7.
Pr=7 :- order("Margie", _, Pr).

% 3. The competitor who ordered spaghetti paid 2 dollars more than the competitor who ordered taglioni.
Pr1 = Pr2 + 2 :- order(_, "spaghetti", Pr1), order(_, "taglioni", Pr2).

% 4. Of the runner who paid $9 and the contestant who ordered fettuccine, one was Margie and the other was Velma.
